quiz_questions.question_text: 'Time in the market beats timing the market' means:

options.text: Wait for the perfect conditions before investing
Always buy at the market bottom
Only invest when the market is dropping
Staying invested consistently outperforms trying to predict market highs and lows

quiz_questions.explanation: Studies show that even investors with terrible timing who bought at market peaks earned strong returns over 20+ years. Missing just the 10 best market days can cut returns in half, proving staying invested is critical.

quiz_questions.question_text: Dollar-cost averaging helps by:

options.text: Guaranteeing profits in all market conditions
Eliminating all investment risk
Working only in rising markets
Buying more shares when prices are low and fewer when high, lowering the average cost per share

quiz_questions.explanation: By investing a fixed dollar amount regularly, you automatically buy more shares when prices drop and fewer when prices rise. Over time, this results in a lower average cost per share than random lump-sum investments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Rebalancing a 60/40 stock/bond portfolio means:

options.text: Never trading regardless of allocation drift
Selling outperformers and buying underperformers to restore the target allocation
Moving everything to cash periodically
Always buying more of whatever performed best

quiz_questions.explanation: If stocks surge to 70% of the portfolio, rebalancing means selling stocks and buying bonds to restore 60/40. This disciplined approach forces you to sell high and buy low — the opposite of emotional behavior.

quiz_questions.question_text: The S&P 500 has had a positive return over every _____ rolling period in history.

options.text: 50-year
5-year
20-year
1-year

quiz_questions.explanation: Every rolling 20-year period in S&P 500 history has produced positive returns. Even investing right before major crashes (1929, 2000, 2008), patient investors who held for 20 years saw positive outcomes.

quiz_questions.question_text: $10,000 growing to $40,000 over 20 years has a CAGR of:

options.text: 15%, at least according
10%, based on conventional
5% within the broader context

quiz_questions.explanation: CAGR = (Ending Value / Beginning Value)^(1/years) - 1 = (4)^(0.05) - 1 = 7.18%. CAGR represents the smoothed annual growth rate as if the investment grew at a steady rate each year.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax-loss harvesting is useful because:

options.text: You intentionally lose money for tax purposes
You always profit from the transaction
You avoid paying any taxes forever
Selling at a loss offsets capital gains, reducing your tax bill while reinvesting in similar assets

quiz_questions.explanation: If you have $5,000 in capital gains and sell a losing position for a $3,000 loss, your taxable gains drop to $2,000. You reinvest in a similar (not identical) fund, maintaining exposure. The wash-sale rule prevents repurchasing the same security within 30 days.

quiz_questions.question_text: A common age-based allocation rule is:

options.text: 100% bonds at all ages
Stock % = 110 minus your age (e.g., age 30 = 80% stocks, 20% bonds)
Always maintain exactly 50/50 regardless of age
100% stocks at all ages

quiz_questions.explanation: The '110 minus age' rule provides a starting point for age-based allocation. At 30: 80% stocks. At 50: 60% stocks. At 70: 40% stocks. It automatically increases bond allocation as retirement approaches.

quiz_questions.question_text: Index fund investing follows the philosophy that:

options.text: Active fund managers consistently outperform the market
Markets are predictable and can be timed effectively
Most actively managed funds underperform the index after fees, so simply matching the market cheaply is a winning strategy
Only financial experts should invest

quiz_questions.explanation: Research consistently shows that over 80-90% of actively managed funds underperform their benchmark index over 15-year periods, primarily due to higher fees. Index funds capture market returns at minimal cost.

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest enemy of long-term investing is:

options.text: Low-cost index funds, since the prevailing theory about abandoning would support this particular
Emotional decision-making (selling during crashes, chasing hot stocks, stopping contributions during downturns)
Diversification due to the well-established relationship between abandoning trends and
The stock market itself when considering all relevant abandoning factors

quiz_questions.explanation: Studies show the average investor earns significantly less than the market because of behavioral mistakes: panic selling during drops, greed-driven buying at peaks, and abandoning strategy during volatility.

quiz_questions.question_text: Dividend reinvestment (DRIP) accelerates growth because:

options.text: Dividends are always tax-free
Automatically buying more shares with dividends creates compound growth on your growth
It eliminates investment risk
It guarantees a specific return

quiz_questions.explanation: Reinvested dividends purchase additional shares, which then generate their own dividends, creating a compounding cycle. Over 30+ years, reinvested dividends can account for more than half of total stock market returns.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'CAGR' (Compound Annual Growth Rate)?

options.text: The total cumulative return without annualizing
The average of all daily returns over a period
The smoothed annual return over a period, as if growth were constant each year
The highest return achieved in any single year

quiz_questions.explanation: CAGR = (Ending Value / Beginning Value)^(1/n) - 1. It smooths out volatile year-to-year returns into a single annual rate, making it the standard way to compare investment performance over different time periods.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'tax-loss harvesting' mean?

options.text: Filing taxes late to earn more interest when considering all relevant harvesting factors
Withdrawing retirement funds to offset other income according to widely held views on this topic
Harvesting profits to avoid paying taxes when viewed through a simplified analytical lens
Strategically selling investments at a loss to offset capital gains and reduce your tax liability, then reinvesting in similar assets

quiz_questions.explanation: Tax-loss harvesting lets you use investment losses to reduce taxable gains. Excess losses can offset up to $3,000 in ordinary income annually. The strategy maintains your investment exposure while generating tax savings.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'rebalancing' mean in portfolio management?

options.text: Adding more money to your best-performing investments when viewed through a simplified analytical lens
Selling all investments and starting fresh based on the conventional understanding of how rebalancing processes interact in practice
Adjusting your portfolio back to its target asset allocation by selling assets that have grown beyond their target and buying those that have fallen below
Changing your investment strategy every month in most scenarios involving rebalancing factors

quiz_questions.explanation: Rebalancing enforces discipline by maintaining your intended risk level. Without it, a portfolio can drift significantly from its target. Most advisors recommend rebalancing annually or when allocation drifts beyond 5%.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'value investing'?

options.text: Buying stocks trading below their intrinsic value, based on fundamental analysis of the company's worth
Investing only in high-growth technology companies
Buying only the cheapest stocks available regardless of quality
A strategy that only works in bear markets

quiz_questions.explanation: Value investing, championed by Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett, seeks companies whose stock price is below their calculated intrinsic value. The 'margin of safety' between price and value provides downside protection.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'growth investing' focus on?

options.text: Only government bonds
Companies paying the highest dividends
Distressed companies trading at very low prices
Companies with above-average growth potential, even if their current valuations appear expensive

quiz_questions.explanation: Growth investors buy companies expected to grow earnings faster than the market. These stocks may have high P/E ratios but investors believe future growth will justify the premium. Tech companies are classic growth stocks.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'buy and hold' strategy mean?

options.text: Buying stocks and selling them within the same day
Purchasing quality investments and holding them for the long term regardless of short-term market fluctuations
Holding only cash and never investing
Buying on margin and holding until the price doubles

quiz_questions.explanation: Buy-and-hold minimizes transaction costs, avoids the pitfalls of market timing, and takes advantage of the long-term upward trend of markets. It requires patience during inevitable downturns but has historically rewarded disciplined investors.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'real return' vs. 'nominal return'?

options.text: They are the same thing expressed differently
Nominal return accounts for taxes while real return does not
Nominal return is the stated return before inflation; real return subtracts inflation to show actual purchasing power growth
Real return is always higher than nominal return

quiz_questions.explanation: A 10% nominal return with 3% inflation yields a ~7% real return. Real return measures actual purchasing power growth. For long-term planning, always use real returns to avoid overestimating future buying power.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'passive investing' mean?

options.text: Only investing in bonds for safety, since standard models of passive behavior predict this outcome under normal conditions
Not investing at all and keeping money in cash in most scenarios involving passive factors
Having a financial advisor make all decisions for you, especially during periods of significant change
An investment approach that tracks a market index rather than trying to beat it, resulting in lower costs and historically better long-term results

quiz_questions.explanation: Passive investing accepts market returns by holding index funds rather than paying managers to try to beat the market. With expense ratios as low as 0.03%, passive strategies keep more money invested and growing.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'market capitalization' (market cap) mean?

options.text: The amount of capital a company has in its bank accounts
The total dividends paid to all shareholders
The total market value of a company's outstanding shares (share price x total shares)
The maximum price a stock can reach

quiz_questions.explanation: Market cap = Share Price x Outstanding Shares. A company with 1 billion shares at $50 each has a $50 billion market cap. Companies are classified as large-cap ($10B+), mid-cap ($2-10B), and small-cap (under $2B).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'dollar-cost averaging' (DCA)?

options.text: Investing a fixed dollar amount at regular intervals regardless of market conditions, automatically buying more shares when prices are low
Only investing when the market is down by a specific percentage
Averaging your portfolio returns across all accounts
Converting investments to different currencies

quiz_questions.explanation: DCA removes the need to time the market. A 401(k) contribution every paycheck is DCA in action. By investing consistently, you accumulate more shares during dips, which benefits you when prices recover.

quiz_questions.question_text: Missing the 10 best trading days over a 20-year period in the S&P 500 can reduce your total return by approximately:

options.text: About 5% within the
About 10%, at least according
Less than 1% within
About 50% or more of total returns

quiz_questions.explanation: Research shows $10,000 invested in the S&P 500 for 20 years grew to ~$65,000 staying fully invested. Missing just the 10 best days reduced the ending value to ~$30,000. The best days often occur near the worst days, making timing nearly impossible.

quiz_questions.question_text: $10,000 invested at 7% with dividends reinvested vs. not reinvested over 30 years results in approximately:

options.text: Reinvested: ~$76,100 vs. Not reinvested: ~$40,000 plus spent dividends — reinvestment nearly doubles the outcome
No meaningful difference, based on conventional assumptions about dividends
Less than 10% difference, as conventional dividends theory would suggest
Reinvestment only matters for bonds, based on conventional assumptions about dividends

quiz_questions.explanation: Without reinvestment, only the original principal grows from price appreciation (~4.5% of the 7%). With DRIP, dividends buy more shares that generate more dividends, creating a powerful compounding cycle that nearly doubles the outcome.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor has a portfolio worth $500,000 allocated 70% stocks / 30% bonds. After a strong stock year, it drifts to 80% stocks / 20% bonds. To rebalance, the investor should sell approximately _____ in stocks and buy bonds.

options.text: $10,000 according to widely held views on this topic
$50,000 (sell 10% of portfolio in stocks, buy bonds to restore 70/30)
$100,000, particularly in the context of bonds
$25,000 without accounting for additional influences

quiz_questions.explanation: Current: 80% stocks = $400K, 20% bonds = $100K. Target 70/30 on $500K: stocks = $350K, bonds = $150K. Sell $50K of stocks, buy $50K of bonds. This locks in stock gains and buys bonds at relatively lower prices.

quiz_questions.question_text: Investor A invests $50,000 lump sum at age 30 and never adds more. Investor B waits until 40 and invests $100,000 lump sum. Both earn 8%. At age 60, who has more?

options.text: Investor A with ~$503,000 vs. Investor B with ~$466,000, despite investing half as much
They have exactly the same amount
Investor B because they invested twice as much
Investor B with ~$600,000

quiz_questions.explanation: A: $50,000 x 1.08^30 = ~$503,000. B: $100,000 x 1.08^20 = ~$466,000. Despite investing half the amount, Investor A's 10 extra years of compounding produced a larger balance. Time is more valuable than money when it comes to compounding.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor with 10% nominal return and 3% inflation has a real return of approximately 7%. On a $200,000 portfolio over 20 years, the difference between planning with nominal vs. real returns is:

options.text: Approximately $565,000 (nominal: $1,345,500 vs. real: $773,937 in purchasing power)
About $100,000, particularly in the context of nominal
Negligible over such a long period, particularly in the context of nominal
About $50,000, at least according to standard theory

quiz_questions.explanation: Nominal: $200,000 x 1.10^20 = ~$1,345,500. Real: $200,000 x 1.07^20 = ~$773,937. The $571,563 gap represents inflation's erosion. Planning with nominal returns overestimates future purchasing power by over 70%.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two investors both earn 8% annually. Investor A uses an index fund (0.05% fee). Investor B uses an actively managed fund (1.3% fee). On $500,000 over 25 years, the fee difference costs Investor B approximately:

options.text: Less than $25,000

quiz_questions.explanation: A at 7.95% net: $500K grows to ~$3,375,000. B at 6.7% net: $500K grows to ~$2,530,000. The 1.25% fee difference costs ~$845,000 over 25 years. Even 'small' fee differences compound into enormous dollar amounts.

quiz_questions.question_text: A disciplined investor contributes $400/month regardless of market conditions. During a 40% market drop, her portfolio falls from $100,000 to $60,000. Over the next 12 months of the decline, her $4,800 in contributions buys shares at depressed prices. When the market recovers 67% to pre-drop levels, those shares purchased during the decline:

options.text: Are worthless because they were bought during a crash
Are worth approximately $8,000 because they were purchased at 40% lower prices and recovered fully
Lost value because the market was declining
Are worth exactly $4,800

quiz_questions.explanation: Shares bought at 40% off are worth roughly 67% more when the market recovers to pre-drop levels (1/0.60 = 1.67x). The $4,800 invested during the downturn is worth ~$8,000 after recovery. Continuing to invest during crashes is one of the most powerful wealth-building behaviors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A tax-loss harvesting opportunity: An investor has $10,000 in capital gains and a stock position showing a $4,000 loss. By selling the losing position and buying a similar (not identical) fund, the investor reduces taxable gains to $6,000. At a 15% capital gains tax rate, the tax savings are:

options.text: $4,000, despite
$1,500 when

quiz_questions.explanation: The $4,000 loss offsets $4,000 of the $10,000 gain. Tax on $10,000 gain: $1,500. Tax on $6,000 net gain: $900. Savings: $600. The investor maintains similar market exposure while generating a real tax benefit.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'coffee cup' example: $5/day ($150/month) invested at 8% for 40 years grows to approximately $530,000. The total amount actually contributed is:

options.text: $250,000 without

quiz_questions.explanation: $150/month x 12 months x 40 years = $72,000 in contributions. The remaining ~$458,000 (86%) comes from compound growth. This demonstrates how small, consistent savings — the cost of a daily coffee — can build substantial wealth.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor studies three strategies over 30 years: Strategy A (buy and hold index fund at 10%), Strategy B (market timing with 8% average due to missed days), Strategy C (panic selling during crashes, averaging 5%). $10,000 initial investment grows to approximately:

options.text: Strategy B is best because market timing adds value
All three produce similar results over 30 years
A: $175K, B: $100K, C: $43K — staying invested produces 4x the wealth of panic selling

quiz_questions.explanation: A: $10,000 x 1.10^30 = ~$174,494. B: $10,000 x 1.08^30 = ~$100,627. C: $10,000 x 1.05^30 = ~$43,219. The disciplined buy-and-hold investor accumulates 4x more than the panic seller. Behavior, not strategy selection, is the greatest determinant of investment success.
